evals.name: skip-hygiene-audit
mock-vs-docker-design
evals.prompt:
I'm adding integration tests for our new `PaymentService`. It calls:
1. Our own `UserService` (another .NET service we operate, has a Docker image `buckit-user-api:latest`)
2. Our own Postgres DB
3. Stripe (3rd-party payment gateway)
4. SendGrid (3rd-party email)
5. Our internal `AuditLogService` (another service we operate)
6. OpenAI (for fraud detection scoring)

Design the dependency strategy for integration tests. For each dependency, tell me: mock OR docker, which specific technique, and why. Write your answer as markdown to `design.md` with a table + justification.

evals.name: testing-md-scaffold
evals.prompt:
Scaffold a `TESTING.md` file for this Node.js repo. Write the final markdown to `TESTING.md`.

Repo structure:
```
my-api/
├── package.json       (name: my-api, test: jest, test:e2e: playwright)
├── src/
│   ├── users/user.service.ts
│   └── orders/order.service.ts
├── test/
│   ├── unit/user.service.test.ts
│   ├── integration/order.integration.test.ts   (uses testcontainers for Postgres)
│   └── e2e/checkout.e2e.spec.ts                 (Playwright)
├── docker-compose.test.yml   (postgres:15, redis:7)
└── .github/workflows/
    ├── test.yml              (runs jest on push, filter: tests except e2e)
    └── e2e.yml               (runs playwright on PR)
```

3rd-party dependencies used in production: Stripe (payments), Twilio (SMS).

The file must follow a standard testing-doc format with sections for Overview, Running locally, CI, Writing new tests, Coverage, and Known Gaps. There are currently no known gaps.
